terms.definition: A coin worth ten pence; ten of these make one pound.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I have two 10p coins — that is 20 pence.

terms.definition: A coin worth one penny; the smallest value coin in British currency.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How many 1p coins do you need to make 10p?

terms.definition: Flat shapes with only length and width; they have no depth or thickness.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A circle is a 2-D shape — it is flat.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 2-D (flat) with 3-D (solid).

terms.definition: A coin worth twenty pence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 20p coin is the same as two 10p coins.

terms.definition: A coin worth two pence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Five 2p coins make 10p.

terms.definition: Solid shapes that have length, width, and height; they take up space.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A ball is a 3-D shape — you can hold it.
terms.common_errors: Calling a drawing of a cube a 3-D shape.

terms.definition: A coin worth fifty pence; two of these make one pound.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 50p coin is half of £1.

terms.definition: A coin worth five pence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Two 5p coins make 10p.

terms.definition: Higher than; in a position over something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the red cube above the blue cube.

terms.definition: To combine two or more numbers together to find a total.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Add 3 and 5 to make 8.

terms.definition: The mathematical operation of combining numbers to find their total or sum.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use addition to find how many altogether: 4 + 6 = 10.

terms.definition: Coming next or later in a sequence of numbers or events.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What number comes after 7? It is 8.

terms.definition: The part of the day from noon (12:00) to evening.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: We have PE in the afternoon.

terms.definition: The total when everything is combined; the result of adding all amounts together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I have 3 red and 4 blue cubes — 7 altogether.

terms.definition: Turning in the opposite direction to clock hands — from right to left when viewed from above.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Turn anticlockwise — that means the opposite way to clock hands.
terms.common_errors: Confusing anticlockwise with clockwise.

terms.definition: Objects arranged in equal rows and columns, used to show multiplication and division.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make an array with 3 rows of 4 counters to show 3 × 4.
terms.common_errors: Making rows of different lengths.

terms.definition: Moving from a larger number to a smaller number; counting down.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count backwards from 10: 10, 9, 8, 7...

terms.definition: A device for comparing the mass of objects, or the state when both sides are equal.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the balance to find which object is heavier.

terms.definition: Coming just in front of a number or earlier in time.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What number comes before 10? It is 9.

terms.definition: At the back of; in a position further away.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The teddy is behind the box.

terms.definition: Lower than; underneath something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the green cube below the red cube.

terms.definition: In the space separating two things; in the middle of two values.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What number is between 5 and 7?

terms.definition: The lowest part or position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Start counting from the bottom of the number line.

terms.definition: How much a container can hold, measured in litres or millilitres.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The capacity of this jug is 1 litre.
terms.common_errors: Confusing capacity (how much it can hold) with volume (how much is in it).

terms.definition: A unit of length; there are 100 centimetres in one metre. Written as cm.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The pencil is 15 centimetres long.
terms.common_errors: Starting from 1 instead of 0 on a ruler.

terms.definition: A perfectly round flat shape where every point on the edge is the same distance from the centre.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a circle around the number 5.
terms.common_errors: Calling ovals or ellipses circles.

terms.definition: A device that shows the time, either with hands (analogue) or digits (digital).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Look at the clock — what time does it show?

terms.definition: Turning in the same direction as clock hands — from left to right when viewed from the front.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Turn clockwise — the same way the hands on a clock move.
terms.common_errors: Confusing clockwise with anticlockwise.

terms.definition: A small round piece of metal used as money.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which coins make 20p?

terms.definition: Vertical arrangements of objects or numbers going from top to bottom.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This array has 4 columns and 3 rows.
terms.common_errors: Confusing columns (vertical) with rows (horizontal).

terms.definition: To put numbers or groups together to find the total.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Combine 4 and 3 — how many altogether?

terms.definition: To look at two or more numbers or objects to find which is bigger, smaller, longer, shorter, etc.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Compare 8 and 12 — which number is greater?

terms.definition: An object that holds liquids or other materials, used when measuring capacity.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which container holds the most water?

terms.definition: The point where two edges of a shape meet.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A square has 4 corners.

terms.definition: The mathematical name for a corner — the point where two or more edges meet on a shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A triangle has 3 corners, which we also call vertices.

terms.definition: To say numbers in order, or to find out how many objects there are.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count the cubes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
terms.common_errors: Counting an object more than once or missing one.

terms.definition: Starting at a number and counting in decreasing steps to find a smaller number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count back 3 from 10: 10, 9, 8, 7.
terms.common_errors: Including the starting number in the count.

terms.definition: Saying the multiples of 5 in order: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count in fives: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

terms.definition: Saying the multiples of 10 in order: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count in tens: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50.

terms.definition: Saying the multiples of 2 in order: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and so on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count in twos: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10.

terms.definition: Starting at a number and counting forward to add more.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To work out 7 + 3, count on from 7: 8, 9, 10.
terms.common_errors: Counting the starting number as the first jump.

terms.definition: A 3-D shape with 6 identical square faces, 12 edges, and 8 vertices.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A dice is the shape of a cube.
terms.common_errors: Calling any box shape a cube — a cube must have all square faces.

terms.definition: A 3-D shape with 6 rectangular faces, 12 edges, and 8 vertices; like a box.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A cereal box is the shape of a cuboid.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cuboid with cube — a cuboid has rectangular faces, a cube has all square faces.

terms.definition: Having a smooth rounded shape; not straight.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A circle has a curved edge.

terms.definition: The seven named days that make up a week: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you say all the days of the week in order?
terms.common_errors: Getting the order wrong or forgetting a day.

terms.definition: To make smaller or reduce in value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If you take away 2, the number decreases.

terms.definition: The face value of a coin or note (e.g. 1p, 2p, 5p, 10p, 20p, 50p, £1, £2).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Sort the coins by denomination — put all the 10p coins together.

terms.definition: The result of subtracting one number from another; how much more or less one number is than another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The difference between 8 and 3 is 5.
terms.common_errors: Confusing difference with just subtraction — difference can also mean comparing.

terms.definition: A single number symbol from 0 to 9.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The number 14 has two digits: 1 and 4.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a digit with a number — 14 is a number made of two digits.

terms.definition: The way something is facing or moving, such as left, right, up, down, forwards, or backwards.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Move 3 steps in this direction.

terms.definition: How far apart two objects or points are.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the distance between the two trees?

terms.definition: To share a quantity into groups that all have the same number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Divide 10 counters equally into 2 groups — each group has 5.
terms.common_errors: Making unequal groups.

terms.definition: Twice as many; the result of adding a number to itself.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Double 4 is 8 because 4 + 4 = 8.
terms.common_errors: Confusing double with adding 2.

terms.definition: The process of making a number twice as big by adding it to itself.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Doubling 5 gives us 10.

terms.definition: In a lower direction or position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count down from 10.

terms.definition: Every one; used when distributing equally.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Give 3 sweets to each child.

terms.definition: A straight line where two faces of a 3-D shape meet.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A cube has 12 edges.
terms.common_errors: Confusing edges with sides — edges are on 3-D shapes, sides on 2-D shapes.

terms.definition: The number 8.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count the pencils — there are eight.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the spelling of eight with ate.

terms.definition: The number 18; one ten and eight ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Eighteen is between seventeen and nineteen.
terms.common_errors: Confusing eighteen (18) with eighty (80).

terms.definition: The number 11; one ten and one more.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is one more than ten? Eleven.
terms.common_errors: Writing 11 as 101 (one-zero-one).

terms.definition: Containing nothing; having no objects or liquid inside.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The box is empty — there are zero cubes.

terms.definition: The same in amount, size, or value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Both towers have 5 bricks — they are equal.

terms.definition: Groups that all contain the same number of objects.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put 12 cubes into equal groups of 3.
terms.common_errors: Making groups of different sizes.

terms.definition: Pieces of a whole that are all exactly the same size.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Cut the sandwich into 4 equal parts.
terms.common_errors: Making parts that are different sizes.

terms.definition: Having the same value as; shown by the = symbol.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5 + 3 is equal to 8.

terms.definition: The symbol = showing that two values are the same.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 4 + 2 equals 6.
terms.common_errors: Thinking equals only means 'the answer is'.

terms.definition: A mathematical sentence with an equals sign showing that two sides have the same value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 + 4 = 7 is an equation.

terms.definition: Having the same value, even though it looks different.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5p + 5p is equivalent to 10p.

terms.definition: A sensible guess at an amount or answer, close to the actual value but not exact.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Estimate how many cubes are in the jar before counting.
terms.common_errors: Thinking an estimate must be exact.

terms.definition: A number that can be divided into 2 equal groups with nothing left over; ends in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 8 is even because you can share 8 into 2 equal groups of 4.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 0 is odd — it is even.

terms.definition: The time of day from late afternoon until bedtime.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: We eat dinner in the evening.

terms.definition: A flat surface on a 3-D shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A cube has 6 faces and they are all squares.
terms.common_errors: Confusing faces with edges.

terms.definition: When a quantity is divided equally so everyone gets the same amount.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If we share 10 biscuits between 5 children, a fair share is 2 each.
terms.common_errors: Not making shares equal.

terms.definition: A long distance away.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How far can you throw the ball?

terms.definition: A smaller number of countable things.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are fewer red cubes than blue cubes.

terms.definition: The number 15; one ten and five ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count on 5 from 10: fifteen.
terms.common_errors: Confusing fifteen (15) with fifty (50).

terms.definition: To put liquid or objects into a container until it is full.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Fill the jug with water.

terms.definition: Coming before all others in order; the position at the start.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The first number in the sequence is 2.

terms.definition: The number 5.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Show me five fingers.

terms.definition: Having no thickness; smooth and level, like a 2-D shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A circle is flat — it is a 2-D shape.

terms.definition: Moving ahead in order from smaller to larger numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count forwards from 1: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

terms.definition: The number 4.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are four legs on the table.
terms.common_errors: Confusing four (number) with for (preposition).

terms.definition: A whole divided into four pieces that are all the same size; each part is a quarter.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Cut the pizza into four equal parts — each piece is one quarter.
terms.common_errors: Making parts that are not the same size.

terms.definition: The number 14; one ten and four ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are fourteen children in the group.
terms.common_errors: Confusing fourteen (14) with forty (40).

terms.definition: Containing as much as possible; no more can be added.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The cup is full of water.

terms.definition: A unit for measuring how heavy something is. Written as g.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The apple weighs about 100 grams.

terms.definition: Bigger in value or amount.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 12 is greater than 8.

terms.definition: Having a higher value; shown by the > symbol.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 15 is greater than 10.
terms.common_errors: Confusing > with <.

terms.definition: A set of objects collected together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the cubes into a group of 5.

terms.definition: One of two equal parts of a whole.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Cut the apple in half — each piece is the same size.
terms.common_errors: Thinking halves don't need to be equal.

terms.definition: A container filled to the middle point — containing half its capacity.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The bottle is half full.

terms.definition: A time 30 minutes after the hour, when the minute hand points to 6.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: It is half past three — the time is 3:30.
terms.common_errors: Reading the wrong hour when the hour hand is between two numbers.

terms.definition: A rotation of 180 degrees — turning to face the opposite direction.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make a half turn — now you are facing the other way.
terms.common_errors: Confusing half turn with quarter turn.

terms.definition: To divide something into two equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Halve 10 — each half is 5.

terms.definition: The process of dividing a number or quantity into two equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Halving is the opposite of doubling.

terms.definition: Having more mass than something else when compared.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The book is heavier than the pencil.
terms.common_errors: Confusing heavier (more mass) with bigger (more size).

terms.definition: Having the greatest mass among a group of objects.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which bag is the heaviest?

terms.definition: Having a lot of mass; difficult to lift.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The bag of potatoes is heavy.
terms.common_errors: Thinking bigger things are always heavier.

terms.definition: How tall something is, measured from bottom to top.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure the height of the tower.
terms.common_errors: Confusing height (vertical) with length (horizontal).

terms.definition: Contains or can contain a certain amount.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This cup holds about 250 millilitres.

terms.definition: A unit of time equal to 60 minutes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There is 1 hour between 2 o'clock and 3 o'clock.

terms.definition: A question asking for the difference between two amounts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are 8 red and 5 blue — how many more red? The answer is 3.
terms.common_errors: Adding instead of finding the difference.

terms.definition: Ahead of; in a forward position relative to something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The car is in front of the house.

terms.definition: To make bigger or add more to a value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If you add 3, the number increases.

terms.definition: Within a space or boundary.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How many cubes are inside the box?

terms.definition: The opposite operation; addition and subtraction are inverse operations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the inverse: if 5 + 3 = 8, then 8 - 3 = 5.

terms.definition: A unit for measuring mass; 1 kilogram = 1,000 grams. Written as kg.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A bag of flour weighs 1.5 kilograms.

terms.definition: The smallest amount or number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which colour has the least votes?

terms.definition: The direction opposite to right; the side your left hand is on.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Turn to the left.
terms.common_errors: Confusing left and right.

terms.definition: How long something is from one end to the other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure the length of the ribbon.
terms.common_errors: Confusing length with height.

terms.definition: A smaller amount or number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5 is less than 8.

terms.definition: Having a smaller value; shown by the < symbol.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 is less than 7.
terms.common_errors: Confusing < with >.

terms.definition: Having little mass; easy to lift.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The feather is very light.

terms.definition: Having less mass than something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The pencil is lighter than the book.

terms.definition: Having the least mass among a group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which object is the lightest?

terms.definition: A unit for measuring how much liquid a container holds. Written as l.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The bucket holds 5 litres of water.

terms.definition: Having a great length from one end to the other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The snake is very long.

terms.definition: Having greater length than something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The red ribbon is longer than the blue ribbon.

terms.definition: Having the greatest length among a group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which pencil is the longest?

terms.definition: To create a number or shape using given parts or operations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What two numbers make 10?

terms.definition: How much matter an object contains; measured in grams and kilograms.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the scales to find the mass of the apple.
terms.common_errors: Confusing mass with weight — in primary maths they are used interchangeably.

terms.definition: To find out the size, length, mass, or capacity of something using a standard unit.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Measure the length of the table using a ruler.
terms.common_errors: Not starting at zero when using a ruler.

terms.definition: A unit of length equal to 100 centimetres. Written as m.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The classroom is about 10 metres long.

terms.definition: The central position between two points or in a group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5 is in the middle of 3 and 7.

terms.definition: A small unit for measuring liquid capacity; 1,000 ml = 1 litre. Written as ml.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A teaspoon holds about 5 millilitres.

terms.definition: A word meaning subtract or take away; the operation shown by the - symbol.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 10 minus 3 equals 7.

terms.definition: A unit of time; there are 60 minutes in one hour.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The minute hand on the clock moves once every minute.

terms.definition: An unknown number in a number sentence that needs to be found.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the missing number: 4 + __ = 9.

terms.definition: The twelve named divisions of the year: January through December.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are 12 months in a year.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting the order of months.

terms.definition: A greater amount; having a larger value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 8 is more than 5.

terms.definition: The part of the day from when you wake up until noon.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: We have maths in the morning.

terms.definition: The greatest number or amount.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which colour has the most votes?

terms.definition: A number that can be divided by another number with no remainder; a result of a times table.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 10, 20, and 30 are multiples of 10.
terms.common_errors: Confusing multiples with factors.

terms.definition: Close by; a short distance away.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The shop is near the school.

terms.definition: Coming immediately after in order or position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the next number after 15?

terms.definition: The number 9.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I have nine counters.

terms.definition: The number 19; one ten and nine ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Nineteen is one less than twenty.
terms.common_errors: Confusing nineteen (19) with ninety (90).

terms.definition: A piece of paper money, such as a £5, £10, or £20 note.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A £5 note is worth five pounds.

terms.definition: A value used for counting, measuring, or labelling.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What number is this? It is 7.

terms.definition: A pair of numbers that add together to make a given total, especially bonds to 10 or 20.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 and 7 are a number bond to 10.
terms.common_errors: Not knowing all the pairs — children often miss 4+6 or 2+8.

terms.definition: A straight line marked with numbers at equal intervals, used for counting, adding, and subtracting.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the number line to count on 4 from 7.
terms.common_errors: Counting the starting position as a jump.

terms.definition: The word form of a number, such as 'seven' for 7 or 'twelve' for 12.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write the number name for 15: fifteen.
terms.common_errors: Spelling errors in number names.

terms.definition: A written symbol representing a number, such as 1, 2, 3.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write the numeral for seven: 7.
terms.common_errors: Reversing numerals — writing 5 or 3 backwards.

terms.definition: A time shown when the minute hand points to 12 and the hour hand points to a number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: It is 3 o'clock — time for home.
terms.common_errors: Reading the minute hand instead of the hour hand.

terms.definition: A number that cannot be divided into 2 equal groups; ends in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7 is odd because you cannot share 7 equally into 2 groups.

terms.definition: In a higher position resting on something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the red cube on top of the blue cube.

terms.definition: The number 1; the first counting number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There is one apple.

terms.definition: One of two equal parts of a whole; written as 1/2 or ½.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One half of 10 is 5.
terms.common_errors: Making two unequal parts.

terms.definition: The number 100; ten groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Can you count all the way to one hundred?

terms.definition: The number that is 1 smaller than a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One less than 8 is 7.

terms.definition: The number that is 1 greater than a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One more than 8 is 9.

terms.definition: One of four equal parts of a whole; written as 1/4 or ¼.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: One quarter of 12 is 3.
terms.common_errors: Making four unequal parts.

terms.definition: The place-value column for single units (0-9); also called units.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the number 34, the 4 is in the ones place.

terms.definition: To arrange numbers from smallest to largest or largest to smallest.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Order these numbers from smallest to largest: 8, 3, 15, 6.

terms.definition: Beyond a boundary or shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How many cubes are outside the circle?

terms.definition: A piece of a whole; one section when something is divided.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Shade one part of the shape.

terms.definition: A repeating arrangement of numbers, shapes, or colours that follows a rule.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Continue the pattern: red, blue, red, blue, __, __.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising the repeating unit.

terms.definition: The plural of penny; the smaller unit of British money (100 pence = £1).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This pencil costs 30 pence.

terms.definition: The smallest British coin, worth 1p.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A penny is worth 1p.

terms.definition: A position in a number that shows the value of a digit.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The digit 3 is in the tens place.

terms.definition: A word meaning add; the operation shown by the + symbol.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5 plus 3 equals 8.

terms.definition: Where something is located, described using words like above, below, left, right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Describe the position of the teddy — it is on the shelf.

terms.definition: The main unit of British money, worth 100 pence. Written as £.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A £1 coin is worth 100 pence.

terms.definition: To transfer liquid from one container to another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Pour the water into the measuring jug.

terms.definition: The one before; coming immediately before in a sequence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The previous number to 10 is 9.

terms.definition: To combine or add numbers or groups.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put together 4 and 5 — how many altogether?

terms.definition: A 3-D shape with a flat base (polygon) and triangular faces that meet at a point.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A square-based pyramid has 5 faces.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all pyramids have a square base — they can have any polygon base.

terms.definition: One of four equal parts of a whole.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Cut the sandwich into quarters — each piece is 1/4.
terms.common_errors: Making four unequal parts.

terms.definition: A rotation of 90 degrees — a quarter of the way around a full circle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make a quarter turn to the right.
terms.common_errors: Confusing quarter turn with half turn.

terms.definition: To look at and understand a number, scale, or data display.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Read the number on the number line.

terms.definition: A flat shape with 4 straight sides and 4 right angles; opposite sides are equal.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A rectangle has 4 corners and 4 sides.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising a square as a special rectangle.

terms.definition: The direction opposite to left.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Turn to the right.
terms.common_errors: Confusing left and right.

terms.definition: To move by turning over and over; round shapes roll, flat shapes do not.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A sphere can roll but a cube cannot.

terms.definition: To turn a shape around a fixed point.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rotate the shape a quarter turn.

terms.definition: Having a curved shape like a circle or sphere.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A ball is round.

terms.definition: Horizontal lines of objects or numbers going from left to right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This array has 3 rows of 5.
terms.common_errors: Confusing rows (horizontal) with columns (vertical).

terms.definition: A straight measuring tool marked in centimetres and millimetres.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a ruler to measure the line.
terms.common_errors: Not starting from zero on the ruler.

terms.definition: Equal in value or amount.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 + 4 is the same as 7.

terms.definition: An instrument used for measuring mass or weight.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the apple on the scales to find how heavy it is.

terms.definition: A very short unit of time; 60 seconds make 1 minute.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count to 10 — each count is about 1 second.

terms.definition: An ordered list of numbers that follows a rule or pattern.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Continue the sequence: 2, 4, 6, 8, __, __.

terms.definition: The number 7.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are seven days in a week.

terms.definition: The number 17; one ten and seven ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Seventeen is between sixteen and eighteen.
terms.common_errors: Confusing seventeen (17) with seventy (70).

terms.definition: The form or outline of an object, such as a circle, square, or triangle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What shape is this? It has 3 sides — it is a triangle.

terms.definition: To divide a quantity equally among a group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Share 10 sweets between 2 children.
terms.common_errors: Not sharing equally.

terms.definition: To divide a quantity into 2 equal groups.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Share 8 cubes between two children — each gets 4.

terms.definition: The process of dividing a quantity into equal groups.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Sharing 12 equally among 3 gives 4 each.

terms.definition: Having a small length or height.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This pencil is short.

terms.definition: Not as long or tall as something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The red ribbon is shorter than the blue ribbon.

terms.definition: Having the least length or height among a group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which pencil is the shortest?

terms.definition: A straight edge of a 2-D shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A triangle has 3 sides.
terms.common_errors: Confusing sides of 2-D shapes with faces of 3-D shapes.

terms.definition: The number 6.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A dice has six faces.

terms.definition: The number 16; one ten and six ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Sixteen is between fifteen and seventeen.
terms.common_errors: Confusing sixteen (16) with sixty (60).

terms.definition: To move a shape in a straight line without turning it.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Slide the shape 3 squares to the right.

terms.definition: Having a lesser value, size, or amount than something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5 is smaller than 9.

terms.definition: A 3-D shape that has length, width, and height.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A cube is a solid shape.

terms.definition: A perfectly round 3-D shape, like a ball.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A football is the shape of a sphere.
terms.common_errors: Calling a sphere a circle — a circle is 2-D, a sphere is 3-D.

terms.definition: To separate or divide into parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Split 7 into 5 and 2.

terms.definition: A flat shape with 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A square has 4 sides that are all the same length.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a tilted square is a diamond — it is still a square.

terms.definition: To place objects on top of each other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Stack the cubes to make a tower.

terms.definition: Not curved or bent; going in one direction without turning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A square has straight sides.

terms.definition: To take one number away from another to find the difference.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Subtract 3 from 8 to get 5.

terms.definition: The total when two or more numbers are added together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The sum of 4 and 5 is 9.
terms.common_errors: Using 'sum' to mean any calculation — it specifically means addition.

terms.definition: To remove a number from another; subtraction.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 8 take away 3 equals 5.

terms.definition: Having a great height.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The tower is very tall.

terms.definition: Having greater height than something else.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The tree is taller than the fence.

terms.definition: Having the greatest height among a group.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which tower is the tallest?

terms.definition: The number 10; one group of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: We have ten fingers.

terms.definition: The place-value column for groups of ten; the second digit from the right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 34, the 3 is in the tens place — it means 30.
terms.common_errors: Confusing tens with ones.

terms.definition: The number 13; one ten and three ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Thirteen is between twelve and fourteen.
terms.common_errors: Confusing thirteen (13) with thirty (30).

terms.definition: The number 3.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I have three pencils.

terms.definition: A rotation of 270 degrees — three quarters of the way around a full circle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make a three-quarter turn clockwise.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with a quarter turn in the opposite direction.

terms.definition: A measure of when events happen, read from a clock in hours and minutes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What time does school start?

terms.definition: The present day; this day.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What day is it today?

terms.definition: The day after today.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If today is Monday, tomorrow is Tuesday.

terms.definition: The highest part or position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Put the star on top.

terms.definition: The amount you get when everything is added together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The total of 5 + 3 + 2 is 10.

terms.definition: A flat shape with 3 straight sides and 3 corners (vertices).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A triangle has 3 sides.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all triangles must look like arrowheads.

terms.definition: A rotation or change of direction.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make a quarter turn to the left.

terms.definition: The number 12; one ten and two ones.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are twelve months in a year.

terms.definition: The number 20; two groups of ten.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count to twenty.

terms.definition: Two times; the same as doubling.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I clapped twice — that is 2 times.

terms.definition: Double the number; two times as many items.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If I have 4 and you have twice as many, you have 8.
terms.common_errors: Adding 2 instead of doubling.

terms.definition: The number 2.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: I have two hands.
terms.common_errors: Confusing two (number), to (preposition), and too (also).

terms.definition: The ones place in a number; also a word for standard measures (cm, kg, l).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 35, the 5 is in the units column.

terms.definition: A number that has not been found yet; a missing value in a number sentence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the unknown: 4 + ? = 10.

terms.definition: In a higher direction or position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Count up from 1.

terms.definition: How much something is worth, either as a number or as money.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.etymology: From Latin 'valere' (to be strong, be worth)
terms.example_usage: The value of this coin is 50p.

terms.definition: The amount of space a 3-D object takes up, or the amount of liquid in a container.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The volume of water in the jug is 500 ml.
terms.common_errors: Confusing volume with capacity — volume is how much is in, capacity is how much can fit.

terms.definition: How heavy something is; in primary maths, used interchangeably with mass.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: What is the weight of this bag?

terms.definition: The complete thing before it is divided into parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The whole pizza has 8 slices.

terms.definition: A complete rotation of 360 degrees, ending back where you started.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Make a whole turn — you end up facing the same way.

terms.definition: The value of something, especially coins and notes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: How much is this coin worth?

terms.definition: To record a number using digits or words.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write the number fifteen: 15.
terms.common_errors: Reversing digits when writing teen numbers (e.g. 51 for 15).

terms.definition: The day before today.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If today is Wednesday, yesterday was Tuesday.

terms.definition: The number 0; the starting point on a number line, representing nothing or no quantity.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Start at zero on the number line.
terms.common_errors: Not understanding that zero is a number.

terms.definition: A coin worth one pound sterling; equal to 100 pence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A £1 coin is gold-coloured.

terms.definition: A banknote worth ten pounds sterling.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A £10 note is worth ten £1 coins.

terms.definition: A coin worth two pounds sterling.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A £2 coin is worth the same as two £1 coins.

terms.definition: A banknote worth five pounds sterling.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A £5 note is worth five £1 coins.
